root_motivation: Produce a deterministic, falsifiable Rust core for the Daily Receipt art project: given a day's structured summary plus a chosen day-type (workday | quiet | special) plus the content payload supplied by upstream (a haiku triple, a glyph seed, or a stamp id), emit a byte-stable ESC/POS command stream for one ~3-8cm thermal strip. Physical printing, scheduling, and Claude-API haiku generation are EXPLICITLY out of scope; the testable core is the encoder + day-type classifier + glyph renderer that downstream cron/printer wrappers consume. This isolates the failure-prone surface (ESC/POS byte sequences, classifier heuristics, glyph determinism) from the unfalsifiable surface (does this haiku spark joy).
user_persona: jsy on Arch Linux runs `daily-receipt render --summary day.json --content content.json --out strip.escpos`. Downstream a tiny shell wrapper pipes the file to /dev/usb/lp0 or saves it for the day. The Rust core never touches the printer; downstream wrappers and a future `daily-receipt-printer` crate own that.

acceptance_criteria.description: `daily-receipt render --summary <day.json> --content <content.json> --out <path>` writes a non-empty ESC/POS byte stream to <path> for a workday haiku input and exits 0.

Output bytes begin with the ESC/POS init sequence ESC '@' (0x1B 0x40) and end with a feed-and-cut sequence (GS V 0x42 0x00 = 0x1D 0x56 0x42 0x00). This is the printer-handshake contract every strip must honor.
Rendering is deterministic: two `render` invocations with byte-identical summary+content JSON produce byte-identical ESC/POS output (no timestamps, no RNG, no map iteration order leaking in).

Day-type classifier `classify` returns `workday` when summary has >=3 distinct repos OR >=10 commits, `special` when summary.special_stamp_id is set, and `quiet` otherwise. Exhaustive on the three variants; no fourth bucket.

Haiku content must be exactly three lines, each line 1..=40 visible chars. `render` returns an error (exit code 3) when the content payload has the wrong shape for the day-type. No silent truncation, no panic.

Glyph renderer for a quiet day emits a 24x24 monochrome bitmap encoded as the GS '*' raster image command, deterministic from the input seed (u64). Same seed -> identical bitmap bytes; different seeds -> different bitmaps (collision probability on a 100-seed proptest sample below 5%).

Every strip embeds the ISO 8601 date (YYYY-MM-DD) from the summary in the printed bytes verbatim. `render --summary` with date='2026-05-23' produces output that contains the literal ASCII bytes '2026-05-23'.

Stamp lookup: when content.stamp_id is provided for a special day, the renderer looks up the stamp by id in an embedded stamp table and emits its ESC/POS bytes. Unknown stamp_id -> error (exit code 4), not a panic.

scope: ESC/POS encoding (init, text, raster image, feed, cut) targeting 58mm thermal printers
Three day-type renderers: haiku-strip, glyph-strip, stamp-strip
Deterministic glyph generator (24x24 bitmap from u64 seed)
Day-type classifier from a structured DaySummary
CLI subcommands: render, classify, lint (validate a content payload against its day-type)
non_goals: Driving USB / serial / Bluetooth printer hardware (downstream wrapper)
Calling the Claude API to compose haiku (downstream caller supplies content)
Scheduling daily runs (cron/systemd live in the downstream wrapper)
Multiple strips per day (one render call = one strip)
Color output (thermal is monochrome by physics)
Year-end scroll binding ritual (physical, out of software scope)

additional.no_network: true; renderer never touches the network — all content arrives via stdin/JSON file
additional.no_clock: true; the date comes from the summary JSON, never from system clock — preserves AC3 determinism

five_whys_trace.q: Why do you want a Rust crate for the Daily Receipt?
five_whys_trace.a: The art project needs a tactile daily strip, but the byte stream sent to the thermal printer is a finicky binary protocol (ESC/POS) where a single wrong byte produces a smear of garbage paper. That part deserves to be a tested Rust crate so I can trust it across years of unattended cron runs.

five_whys_trace.q: Why isolate just the ESC/POS encoder and not the whole pipeline (cron + Claude API + USB)?
five_whys_trace.a: Each of those other parts is either non-deterministic (Claude API), environment-specific (USB device path, systemd timer), or trivially shellable. Only the encoder + classifier + glyph renderer are pure functions over structured input — exactly the surface that benefits from autobuilder's iterate-and-prove loop. Mixing the others in would make every iteration depend on flaky network or absent hardware.

five_whys_trace.q: Why is determinism (AC3) load-bearing, given each day's strip is a one-shot artifact?
five_whys_trace.a: Two reasons. First: snapshot tests on byte output are the only way to keep a binary protocol honest without a physical printer in the loop. Second: when the printer mis-prints (jam, low battery, thermal head dirty), I need to be able to re-run the same day's render and get the same strip — otherwise the scroll has gaps where dates are missing or duplicated with different content.

five_whys_trace.q: Why three day-types rather than one universal renderer?
five_whys_trace.a: The PRD already names them (workday haiku, quiet-day glyph, special-day stamp) and the visual+content contract differs per type. A universal renderer would smuggle the day-type into per-call config, making the classifier untestable. Three explicit variants keep the type system honest.

five_whys_trace.q: Why a CLI rather than a library that the larger 'daily ritual' daemon imports?
five_whys_trace.a: The downstream wrapper is shell (cron piping to /dev/usb/lp0), not Rust. A CLI with file-in / file-out is the natural seam. The internal modules are still importable as a library by anyone who wants to, but the project's contract is the CLI.

ambiguities_resolved.question: PRD's open question 1: glyph visual vocabulary (hand-drawn primitives, pure generative noise, symbol-from-bigram).
ambiguities_resolved.resolution: v1 picks deterministic generative bitmap from u64 seed — the simplest variant that satisfies AC6 falsifiably. Other vocabularies can be added behind a glyph-style flag in v2.

ambiguities_resolved.question: PRD's open question 3: year-end letter print.
ambiguities_resolved.resolution: Out of scope for v1. v1 owns one strip per render call.

ambiguities_resolved.question: Cluster overlap with PRD-receipt.md and PRD-session-trace-receipt.md.
ambiguities_resolved.resolution: Names overlap but artifacts are unrelated — daily-receipt produces a thermal-paper byte stream; the other two produce JSON gate receipts. No shared code, no shared dependency. Confirmed at intake.

ambiguities_resolved.question: Printer hardware: PRD lists Phomemo M02 / Star TSP100 / Epson TM-T20 (decide on arrival).
ambiguities_resolved.resolution: All three speak ESC/POS-compatible dialects for the init + raster + cut subset used here. The encoder targets the lowest-common-denominator command subset; printer-specific quirks are downstream's problem.
